rationale: Both HLS clauses require the organization to determine internal and external issues relevant to its purpose that affect intended management system outcomes — co-extensive 1:1.
Both HLS clauses require identifying interested parties and their relevant requirements — co-extensive 1:1 at sub-clause level.
Both HLS clauses require determining and documenting the scope of the management system — co-extensive 1:1.
Both HLS clauses require establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving the management system — co-extensive 1:1.
ISO 22301:2019 clause 4.5 (BCMS establishment) is subsumed within 27001:4.4 (ISMS general establishment); 22301 adds BC-specific output requirements beyond the general ISMS establishment language.
Both HLS leadership-and-commitment clauses require top management to demonstrate commitment and accountability for the management system — co-extensive 1:1.
Both require top management to establish a domain policy (BC policy vs. IS policy) with identical structural requirements — co-extensive 1:1 at this HLS level.
Both HLS clauses require top management to assign, communicate, and ensure understanding of roles and responsibilities — co-extensive 1:1.

ISO 22301:2019 clause 5.4 (Communicating BC intent) is largely captured by 27001:5.1 top-management commitment language; 22301 places additional explicit BC communication obligations that have no dedicated 27001 sub-clause.
Both HLS planning clauses require determining risks and opportunities to be addressed and planning actions for them — co-extensive 1:1.
Both establish general risk-and-opportunity determination requirements at sub-clause level — co-extensive 1:1.

22301:6.1.2 frames risk assessment in the context of BC disruption likelihood/impact while 27001:6.1.2 frames it for information-security risks; methodology requirements overlap but the risk objects differ.
Both HLS clauses require setting objectives, planning how to achieve them, and maintaining documented information — co-extensive 1:1.
Both 2019/2022 revisions added a Planning-of-Changes sub-clause with identical HLS wording — co-extensive 1:1.
Both HLS support clauses require the organization to determine and provide necessary resources — co-extensive 1:1.
Both require determining necessary competence, ensuring it, taking action, and retaining documented evidence — co-extensive 1:1.
Both require ensuring persons doing relevant work are aware of policy, their contribution, and non-conformity implications — co-extensive 1:1.
Both HLS communication clauses require determining what, when, with whom, and how to communicate — co-extensive 1:1; 22301 adds BC-specific communication plan elements.
Both HLS documented-information clauses share identical structure (7.5.1 general, 7.5.2 creating/updating, 7.5.3 control) — co-extensive 1:1.
Both sub-clauses set general requirements for documented information inclusion — co-extensive 1:1.
Both sub-clauses address creating and updating documented information with identical requirements — co-extensive 1:1.
Both sub-clauses address control of documented information with identical availability, protection, and distribution requirements — co-extensive 1:1.
Both HLS operational-planning-and-control clauses require implementing and controlling the processes to meet requirements — co-extensive 1:1.

22301:8.2 (BIA + risk assessment) is broader than 27001:6.1 because it adds a mandatory Business Impact Analysis (MTPD, MBCO, RTO, RPO determination) that has no direct 27001 equivalent; 27001 risk assessment alone does not cover BIA deliverables.
22301:8.2.1 general BIA/RA requirement relates to 27001:6.1.1 risk-and-opportunity planning but 22301 uniquely mandates BIA outputs (MTPD/MBCO) beyond 27001 scope.
22301:8.2.2 Business Impact Analysis adds criticality ranking, MTPD/MBCO/RTO/RPO determination that goes well beyond 27001:8.2 IS risk assessment scope.

22301:8.2.3 risk assessment methodology overlaps with 27001:6.1.2 but 22301 focuses on disruption-likelihood/consequence for activities while 27001 focuses on threat/vulnerability/CIA impact for information assets.

22301:8.3 (BC strategy development including resource, people, data, ICT, supplier and stakeholder strategies) covers far more than 27001:A.5.29 which only requires IS continuity be embedded in BC management processes.
22301:8.3.1 general strategy requirements encompass the full set of continuity solutions; 27001:A.5.29 is a single organisational control requiring IS continuity plans within a BC framework.
22301:8.3.2 identification of strategies covers all resource types (people, facilities, technology, information, supply chain); 27001:A.5.30 narrows focus to ICT readiness only.
22301:8.3.3 selection and approval of strategies includes non-ICT solutions; 27001:A.5.30 is scoped exclusively to ICT continuity (RTO/RPO/availability targets).

22301:8.4 (BC plans and procedures) comprehensively defines crisis-response, communication, BC and recovery plans; 27001:A.5.29 requires only that IS continuity procedures exist within a broader BC plan.
22301:8.4.1 requires documented BC plans covering all prioritised activities; 27001:A.5.29 requires IS continuity specifically — BC plans contain IS continuity as a subset.

22301:8.4.2 (response structure, crisis teams, roles/responsibilities) relates to 27001:A.5.24 (incident management planning) but BC response teams cover operational disruptions beyond security incidents.

22301:8.4.3 (warning and communication procedures) shares communication-during-disruption intent with 27001:A.5.26 (response to information security incidents) but 22301 scope includes all crisis communications.
22301:8.4.4 BC plans cover all continuity dimensions; 27001:A.5.30 requires ICT continuity plans specifically — the 27001 control is a named subset of the 22301 BC plan requirement.
22301:8.4.5 (recovery, restore and return) relates to 27001:A.8.13 (information backup) but 22301 covers full business restoration across all resource types, not only data backup.
22301:8.5 (exercises and testing programme) is broader than 27001:A.5.36 (compliance with IS policies) and 27001:9.1; testing BC plans exercises response capability, not only compliance verification.

22301:8.5 requires periodic testing of BC plans through exercises; 27001:9.1 requires monitoring, measurement and evaluation of ISMS controls — both are performance-verification mechanisms but focus on different outputs.
Both HLS performance-evaluation clauses require determining what, how, and when to monitor/measure/analyse/evaluate — co-extensive 1:1 at clause level.
22301:9.1.1 general performance evaluation mirrors 27001:9.1 monitoring requirements — co-extensive 1:1.
22301:9.1.2 (measurement and evaluation of BCMS effectiveness) is co-extensive with 27001:9.1 analysis-and-evaluation requirements — 1:1 at this granularity.
Both HLS internal-audit clauses require audit programmes, criteria, reporting, and corrective action — co-extensive 1:1.
Both address general internal audit requirements with identical HLS wording — co-extensive 1:1.
Both address internal audit programme management with identical structural requirements — co-extensive 1:1.
Both HLS management-review clauses require periodic review of the management system covering the same input/output categories — co-extensive 1:1.
Both sub-clauses set general management review requirements — co-extensive 1:1.
Both sub-clauses define review inputs with largely identical required items (performance, audit results, stakeholder feedback, risks) — co-extensive 1:1.
Both sub-clauses require management review outputs including decisions on continual improvement and resource needs — co-extensive 1:1.
Both HLS nonconformity-and-corrective-action clauses require reacting, investigating root cause, implementing corrections, and reviewing effectiveness — co-extensive 1:1.
Both HLS continual-improvement clauses require improving suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness — co-extensive 1:1.

22301:10.3 (corrective action) is the same sub-requirement as 27001:10.1 when both standards are read at matching depth; some editions of 22301 merge 10.1/10.2/10.3 differently but content is co-extensive.
